title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: CARB Reports Highest Credit Prices in 22 Weeks; Transfer Volumes Fall WoW
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights

(13th â€“ 19th October 2025)
  • A total of 309,356 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a fall of 74.11% week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades witnessed a fall of 84.61% WoW as 843,839 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price stood at $57.15/MT for Type 1 transfers, recording a 6.92% rise WoW; All Non-Zero transfers saw a fall of 3.69%, priced at $56.64/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price declined by 0.20% WoW; on 21st October, the spot price stood at $54.75/MT, and the benchmark price stood at $55.50/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices rose by 1.73% WoW.D4, D5, and D6 prices rose by 2.02%, 3.06% and 3.03% respectively, WoW.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

The month of October so far, has recorded the highest monthly trading volumes ever observed in the California LCFS. The surge underscores heightened market activity and strong compliance-driven demand. On the policy front, the California Transportation Commission (CTC) has approved $4.9 billion in funding to improve safety and mobility across local streets and state highways. The investment package also prioritizes alternative transportation modes, zero-emission projects, and freight movement enhancements. Additionally, the Innovative Small E-Fleet Pilot Set-Aside Program (ISEF), which had previously closed due to high demand and fund exhaustion, has reopened on October 21, 2025. Eligible California fleets can access the ISEF Purchaser webpage to review program details and submit applications for participation.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_606272" align="alignnone" width="768"] Table 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 transfer prices recorded a 6.92% WoW increase, rising from $53.45 to $57.15 this week, and all non-zero transfer prices fell from $58.81 to $56.64, recording a decrease of 3.69%. Type 1 trades witnessed 309,356 credits exchanged, recording a 74.11% fall WoW. The total transaction volumes for all non-zero trades saw a decline of 84.61% WoW, reaching 843,839 credits. [caption id="attachment_606273" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption]

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_606274" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices fell by 0.20% WoW, reaching $55.15/MT. On 21st October, the spot price stood at $54.75/MT. The benchmark price, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $55.50/MT on 21st October.

LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_606275" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 77 trades were reported in the market, with 5 high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $100.00 for a Type 1 transaction at a volume of 16,101 credits. The lowest transfer by price was $48.00 per credit for a Type 2 transaction reported for 1,745 credits. The largest volume of transfers involved 150,000 credits for a Type 2 transaction priced at $52.50. The smallest volume of transfer involved 8 credits for a Type 1 transaction priced at $54.00.  

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_606276" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average spot Type 1 price stood at $53.64 in October so far, falling from $53.94 in September. Trade volumes for the month of October stood at 9.14 M as of 21st October, 2025. This month has witnessed the highest Â monthly transaction volumes ever in the history of the program.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_605857" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 13th â€“ 19th October 2025, total VPT fell by 2.91% WoW, with a fall in all non-zero credit prices for the last week.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN Weekly Price Trend

Â RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices rose by 1.73% WoW.D4, D5, and D6 prices rose by 2.02%, 3.06% and 3.03% respectively WoW. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming DatesÂ 

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments This Week

  • Quinbrook Acquires First Canadian Investment With Strategic Stake in Elemental Clean Fuels: Quinbrook, a specialist global investment manager focused exclusively on delivering the infrastructure needed for the energy transition, announced it has acquired a strategic ownership stake in Elemental Clean Fuels (â€œECFâ€), a Canadian clean energy developer advancing scalable renewable fuels and decarbonisation projects across the country. The investment marks Quinbrookâ€™s first investment in Canada, expanding its North American footprint and reinforcing its commitment to building next-generation infrastructure in compelling transition related thematics that support decarbonisation and sustainable impact.
  • The Department of Ecology Has Adopted Amendments to Chapter 173-423 WAC, Clean Vehicles Program.: Ecology has adopted amendments to our Clean Vehicles Program Rules (Chapter 173-423 WAC) to incorporate changes to the Advanced Clean Trucks and Heavy-Duty Low NOx Omnibus regulations adopted by the California Air Resources Board (CARB). These regulations are an essential policy tool for addressing climate change and improving air quality in Washington. These amendments respond to industry concerns by easing compliance requirements for the heaviest vehicles. They also streamline implementation and maintain consistency with state and federal laws.Â  Advanced Clean Trucks applies to medium- and heavy-duty engine and vehicle manufacturers. It requires manufacturers to gradually sell more new zero-emission models in Washington through model year 2035 or buy credits from manufacturers who exceed sales requirements. Credits give manufacturers flexibility in meeting the zero-emission sales requirements. Heavy-Duty Low NOx Omnibus requires that any new heavy-duty internal combustion engines sold in Washington meet more protective air quality standards, particularly on emissions of oxides of nitrogen and particulate matter, starting in model year 2026.
  • Manufacturer Guidance Update: Washington's Vehicle Emissions Standards: Ecology is extending its pause for portions of Advanced Clean Cars II and Advanced Clean Trucks in an update to its regulatory guidance. In response to recent federal actions, Ecology paused portions of Advanced Clean Cars II, Advanced Clean Trucks, and Heavy-Duty Low NOx Omnibus implementation for six months starting on June 6, 2025. This update extends that pause through June 6, 2026. This update maintains the original guidance's pause on Heavy-Duty Low NOx Omnibus for all of model year 2026.Ecology has also finalized our rulemaking for amendments to Advanced Clean Trucks and Heavy-Duty Low NOx Omnibus. Developments at the federal level do not change Washingtonâ€™s commitment to transitioning to cleaner vehicles. The state has legal limits on greenhouse gas emissions between now and 2050 which cannot be met without addressing transportation â€” our biggest single source of greenhouse gas emissions. Governor Ferguson and Ecology recently announced ZEVergreen, an effort to drive continued adoption of affordable, clean transportation in Washington.
  • California's Innovative Small E-Fleet program Reopening This Month: After being closed due to exhaustion of funds driven by high demand, ISEF is set to reopen on October 21, 2025. California fleets can visit the ISEF Purchaser webpage to view offerings and apply to participate in ISEF. Guidelines to participate in ISEF are available through Appendix F of HVIP's Fiscal Year 24-25 Implementation Manual.
  • Ecology Adopts Amendments to Chapter 173-424 WAC, Clean Fuels Program Rule: The Washington State Department of Ecology (Ecology) has adopted amendments to the Clean Fuels Program Rule, Chapter 173-424 WAC. Many of the adopted changes support greenhouse gas emissions reductions in â€œhard-to-decarbonizeâ€ sectors, such as aviation and heavy-duty trucking. Other changes were adopted based on experiences from the programâ€™s first two years of implementation, best practices used in other states, and input from interested parties expressed through public comments, meetings with Ecology staff, informal workshops, and other engagement opportunities.
